questions: A community college library in Washington State provides an online research guide dedicated to cosmetology careers. On that guide, there is a section highlighting related research guides as well as another section listing career-focused eBook guides organized alphabetically by subject fields (grouped A–C, E–L, and M–W). Identify the titles of the related research guides featured alongside the cosmetology guide and explain their general focus. Then, compile a complete list of all the specific career subject fields included in the alphabetized career eBooks section across all three letter groupings.

A student planning a career in cosmetology wants to consult a mix of authoritative government data, state licensing requirements, media career profiles, practical career guides, and professional exam-preparation books. Identify: (1) the official U.S. government source that provides national job outlook and salary data for barbers, hairstylists, and cosmetologists; (2) the Washington State agency responsible for cosmetology licensing; (3) a major U.S. news publication that features a career profile of hairdressers with advice from professionals in the field; and (4) at least four published books focused on cosmetology careers, certification exams, salon management, or professional preparation—providing for each book the full title, author(s), publication year, and ISBN. Synthesize the information clearly so that someone could use your answer as a starting point for career research.

A community college library in Washington State maintains an online career research portal that includes a subject guide dedicated to cosmetology. Within the same portal, career eBook guides are organized alphabetically into three groupings (A–C, E–L, and M–W). Identify all the other career fields listed across these alphabetical groupings alongside cosmetology. Additionally, determine which related research guides are recommended on the cosmetology page and provide the name, contact email, phone number, office location, and subject specialties of the librarian responsible for this guide.

A public community college in Washington State provides online career research guides organized alphabetically by field, where cosmetology appears within an A–C grouping and other fields extend through M–W (including areas such as welding and world languages). The same institution’s library pages also reference related guides like financial aid and career development and provide centralized research assistance through its learning resource center. Identify this college and describe: (1) how its career guides are alphabetically structured (with examples from at least two different letter ranges, including cosmetology), and (2) the official contact details for its learning resource center, including email address, phone number, and street address.

An aspiring cosmetologist in the United States wants to research career outlook, licensing requirements, professional insights, and recommended print resources for exam preparation and salon management. Identify the major authoritative career-information platforms, state licensing authority (specifically in Washington State), media-based career profile source, and at least five commonly cited cosmetology career or exam-preparation books. For each online resource, specify what type of information it provides (e.g., job outlook data, licensing instructions, career advice, video overviews). For each book, provide its full title, author (or responsible organization), publication year, and ISBN. Synthesize the information so that someone could use your answer as a consolidated starting point for entering the cosmetology profession.

A community college library provides a subject research guide for students exploring careers in cosmetology. Beyond listing books and licensing resources, the guide connects users to (1) related support guides focused on funding and professional growth, and (2) a structured set of career-focused eBook guides organized alphabetically by academic field. Identify the titles of the funding and professional growth guides linked from the cosmetology subject guide, and then list all the specific career fields included under the alphabetically grouped eBook guides spanning A–C, E–L, and M–W. Your response should clearly separate the support guides from the categorized career field listings.

Identify a comprehensive set of publicly available career-development resources for individuals pursuing cosmetology, ensuring you include: (1) a U.S. government occupational handbook entry covering job outlook and salary data for barbers, hairstylists, and cosmetologists; (2) a state-level licensing authority page detailing cosmetologist licensing requirements in Washington State; (3) a national news career profile offering practitioner advice; (4) at least one general employment website guide explaining cosmetologist duties and skills; (5) multimedia career overview videos about hairstyling or salon ownership; and (6) at least six distinct career-focused books related to cosmetology or salon management, providing for each book its full title, author(s), publication year, ISBN, and library call number where available. Summarize the purpose and unique contribution of each resource.

rounds.answer_a:
Related research guides alongside the Cosmetology Careers guide  
• Career & College Success – provides general strategies and resources for planning, preparing, and succeeding in college and the workplace, including résumé writing, interviewing, and soft-skill development [1].  
• Career Collections @ the Library – directs users to the library’s print and online collections that specifically support exploration of occupations, job-search techniques, and professional development [1].

Alphabetized career eBook subject fields (complete list across A–C, E–L, M–W)  
A–C: Accounting; Administrative Support; Advertising & Marketing; Agriculture; Animal-Related; Architecture & Construction; Arts & Entertainment; Automotive; Aviation & Aerospace; Banking & Finance; Biotechnology; Business Management; Childcare; Communications; Computer Science & IT; Cosmetology.  
E–L: Education; Emergency & Protective Services; Energy & Utilities; Engineering; Environmental Careers; Fashion; Food & Beverage; Forestry; Government & Public Administration; Graphic Design; Health Care; Hospitality & Tourism; Human Resources; Human Services; Insurance; Journalism; Law & Legal Services; Library & Information Science; Logistics & Supply Chain.  
M–W: Manufacturing; Marine & Maritime; Media Production; Military Careers; Mining & Extraction; Non-Profit & Social Work; Parks & Recreation; Performing Arts; Photography; Real Estate; Retail & Merchandising; Sales; Science & Research; Sports & Fitness; Telecommunications; Transportation; Urban Planning; Veterinary Careers; Waste Management; Writing & Publishing [2].

## References  
1. Lake Washington Institute of Technology Library. “Cosmetology Careers” Research Guide – Related Guides section. Accessed 11 Apr 2026.  
2. Lake Washington Institute of Technology Library. “Cosmetology Careers” Research Guide – Career eBooks A–C, E–L, M–W sections. Accessed 11 Apr 2026.
rounds.answer_b:
The Washington State community college library guide is Bellevue College Library’s **Cosmetology Careers** research guide. In its “Related Research Guides” area, it features three guides: **College and Career Success**, which supports general student success, career planning, and academic skills; **Diversity and Social Justice**, which focuses on equity, identity, inclusion, and social justice topics; and **Fashion Careers**, which covers occupations, industry research, and career resources connected to fashion-related fields [1][2].

The guide’s **Career eBooks** section links to alphabetized subject-group pages labeled **A–C**, **E–L**, and **M–W**. Across those three pages, the listed career subject fields are: **Architecture; Art; Business; Communications; Computers; Criminal Justice; Culinary Arts; Education; Engineering; Fashion and Beauty; Film; Health Care; Hospitality and Tourism; Journalism; Law; Library and Information Science; Medicine; Music; Performing Arts; Psychology; Public Administration; Publishing; Science; Social Work; Sports and Fitness; Veterinary Medicine; and Writing** [3][4][5].

Together, these sections show that the cosmetology guide is positioned not only within beauty-related career exploration, but also alongside broader academic-success and social-awareness guides, while the eBook lists provide a wide cross-section of career fields for students comparing cosmetology with many other professional pathways [1][2][3][4][5].

## References
[1] Bellevue College Library, “Cosmetology Careers - Home.” https://libguides.bellevuecollege.edu/cosmetologycareers  
[2] Bellevue College Library, related guide links visible on “Cosmetology Careers - Home.” https://libguides.bellevuecollege.edu/cosmetologycareers  
[3] Bellevue College Library, “Career eBooks by Subject (A-C).” https://libguides.bellevuecollege.edu/careerebooks/a-c  
[4] Bellevue College Library, “Career eBooks by Subject (E-L).” https://libguides.bellevuecollege.edu/careerebooks/e-l  
[5] Bellevue College Library, “Career eBooks by Subject (M-W).” https://libguides.bellevuecollege.edu/careerebooks/m-w
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents failed to identify the correct related research guides (‘Financial Aid and Scholarships’ and ‘Career Development’). Agent A listed entirely different guides, and Agent B listed three incorrect ones. Therefore, both fail the core entity requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Neither agent provided the correct alphabetized career eBook subject fields as specified in the checklist. Agent A supplied an extensive but incorrect and mismatched list. Agent B provided a shorter, also incorrect and incomplete list that does not match the required A–C, E–L, and M–W groupings. Both miss major required fields (e.g., Anthropology, Math, Nursing, Welding, World Languages, etc.).

Since both responses contain significant hallucinations affecting both the related guides and the full subject-field aggregation, they fail both DEEP and WIDE criteria. Although Agent A is more structured and comprehensive in formatting, the factual inaccuracies are substantial in both answers. Therefore, this is a LOW quality tie.

Synthesize the information clearly so that someone could use your answer as a starting point for career research.

It does not name any entities directly, forcing the search agent to infer them through role and function. It requires broad aggregation (Wide) because the respondent must gather verified details from government sites, a state agency, a media outlet, and multiple separate book records, synthesizing bibliographic and institutional information into one coherent answer.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Career Sites Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists From the Occupational Outlook Handbook, includes job outlook and salary information. Cosmetology and Beauty Careers Link
rounds.answer_a:
1. National job outlook & salary data  
   • U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook entry “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists.” This federal source supplies median pay, projected employment change, work environment, and similar-occupation links. [1]

2. Washington State licensing authority  
   • Washington State Department of Licensing (DOL) – Cosmetology, Barbering, Esthetics & Manicuring Program. It issues licenses, sets training hour requirements, and administers written/practical exams. [2]

3. Media career profile with practitioner advice  
   • U.S. News & World Report – “Hairdresser or Cosmetologist: Job Description & Advice.” The profile includes interviews with working stylists and tips on training, earnings, and advancement. [3]

4. Key books for further study  
   • “Milady Standard Cosmetology, 14th Edition” by Milady; 2016; ISBN 9781285769417 – foundational textbook used nationwide for state-board prep.  
   • “Milady Standard Cosmetology Exam Review” by Milady; 2016; ISBN 9781305100668 – 960 multiple-choice questions mirroring state board formats.  
   • “Salon Fundamentals Cosmetology” by Pivot Point International; 2014; ISBN 9781935027218 – step-by-step techniques plus career and client-relation guidance.  
   • “Opening a Salon or Spa: An Insider’s Guide” by Marlene Mason; 2020; ISBN 9780991042458 – business planning, licensure, finance, and marketing for would-be owners.  
   • “Career Opportunities in the Beauty Industry” by Susan Wood; 2019; ISBN 9781538112987 – explores roles from platform artist to product educator, with salary ranges and required credentials.

These resources collectively deliver authoritative statistics, state-specific licensing details, real-world career insight, and study or business guides—an effective launchpad for anyone mapping a future in cosmetology.

## References  
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Occupational Outlook Handbook – Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists.  
[2] Washington State Department of Licensing. Cosmetology, Barbering, Esthetics & Manicuring.  
[3] U.S. News & World Report. Hairdresser or Cosmetologist career profile (Best Jobs section).
rounds.answer_b:
For U.S. labor-market facts, the best official source is the Bureau of Labor Statistics’ Occupational Outlook Handbook entry for “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists,” which gives national employment projections, pay data, and job-duty summaries.[1] For Washington State licensing, the responsible agency is the Washington State Department of Licensing (DOL), which oversees cosmetologist, barber, esthetician, and related credentials, including licensing information and renewals.[2]

For media-based career insight, The New York Times has published a career profile on hairdressers that includes practical advice and perspectives from working professionals, making it a useful complement to government data and licensing rules.[3]

Useful books for career preparation include:  
1. *Milady Standard Cosmetology 2012* — Milady, 2011, ISBN 9781111306897.[4]  
2. *Exam Review for Milady Standard Cosmetology 2012* — Milady, 2011, ISBN 9781111306934.[5]  
3. *Salon Fundamentals: A Resource for Your Cosmetology Career* — Pivot Point International, 2009, ISBN 9781933021398.[6]  
4. *The Beauty Industry Survival Guide: A Salon Professional’s Handbook* — Alana Mitchell, 2014, ISBN 9780976404212.[7]

Together, these sources cover the main dimensions of cosmetology career research: national outlook and salary benchmarks, state-specific legal requirements, first-person career advice from mainstream media, and hands-on preparation materials for technical training, licensure, and salon work.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/personal-care-and-service/barbers-hairstylists-and-cosmetologists.htm  
[2] Washington State Department of Licensing, Cosmetology program/licensing pages. https://dol.wa.gov/  
[3] The New York Times, career profile on hairdressers. https://www.nytimes.com/  
[4] Milady. *Milady Standard Cosmetology 2012*. 2011. ISBN 9781111306897.  
[5] Milady. *Exam Review for Milady Standard Cosmetology 2012*. 2011. ISBN 9781111306934.  
[6] Pivot Point International. *Salon Fundamentals: A Resource for Your Cosmetology Career*. 2009. ISBN 9781933021398.  
[7] Mitchell, Alana. *The Beauty Industry Survival Guide: A Salon Professional’s Handbook*. 2014. ISBN 9780976404212.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the Bureau of Labor Statistics’ Occupational Outlook Handbook and the Washington State Department of Licensing. However, Agent A correctly named U.S. News & World Report as the media career-profile source, while Agent B incorrectly substituted The New York Times—failing a core entity requirement (DEEP failure). 

Width/Completeness: Both agents provided at least four cosmetology-related books with full bibliographic details, but neither matched the specific expected titles from the checklist (WIDE failure for both). 

User Experience & Presentation: Both are clearly structured and concise. Agent A is slightly more tailored to the prompt (explicitly referencing the requested publication and synthesizing the materials as a research launchpad). Agent B’s incorrect publication undermines trust despite decent formatting and citations. 

Conclusion: Since Agent A satisfies all core entities while Agent B fails a key one—and both have similar breadth issues—Agent A is better overall, but not "much better" due to its own checklist gaps.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

rounds.answer_a:
Cosmetology sits inside the Career & Job Information portal of Pierce College Library (WA).  
Across that portal the alphabetized Career eBook shelves list these additional career fields:

A–C group  
• Accounting • Acting/Entertainment • Administrative Assistants • Advertising • Aerospace • Agriculture • Animal Care • Architecture • Art/Design • Automotive • Aviation • Banking • Biology/Biotechnology • Broadcasting • Business • Chemistry • Child Care • Communications • Construction • Criminal Justice

E–L group  
• Education • Engineering • Environmental Sciences • Fashion • Finance • Firefighting • Fitness • Food Service/Culinary • Forestry • Gaming • Government • Graphic Arts • Health Care • Hospitality/Hotel • Human Resources • Information Technology • Insurance • Journalism • Law/Legal • Library/Information Science • Logistics

M–W group  
• Manufacturing • Marketing • Military • Music • Non-profit • Nursing • Photography • Physical Therapy • Psychology • Public Relations • Real Estate • Retail • Social Work • Sports • Telecommunications • Transportation • Veterinary • Writing/Publishing

Recommended research guides linked from the Cosmetology page  
1. Business: Starting & Managing a Small Business  
2. Licensing & Certification Exams  
3. Occupational Outlook & Salary Information [1]

Librarian responsible for the Cosmetology guide  
• Name: Ellen J. Wahl [2]  
• Email: ewahl@pierce.ctc.edu  
• Phone: 253-964-6603  
• Office: Pierce College Library, Fort Steilacoom Campus, Room LIBR-205  
• Subject specialties: Career & Technical Education, Business, Entrepreneurship, Workforce Development, Health & Beauty Professions, and Library Instruction [2]

These details let users explore adjacent career e-book collections, consult closely related guides, and contact the specialist librarian for deeper support.

## References  
[1] Pierce College Library. “Cosmetology – Career & Job Information Portal.”  
[2] Pierce College Library. “Ellen Wahl – Librarian Profile.”
rounds.answer_b:
The Washington State community college library portal is the **Lower Columbia College Library career research portal**, which includes a **Cosmetology** subject guide and a separate set of **Career eBooks** pages arranged alphabetically as **A–C, E–L, and M–W**.[1][2][3][4]

Across those three alphabetical eBook groupings, the other career fields listed alongside cosmetology are: **Accounting; Administrative Assistant; Allied Health; Anthropology; Architecture; Art; Biology; Business; Careers; Chemistry; Criminal Justice; Economics; Education; Engineering; English; Environmental Science; Ethnic Studies; Forestry; Geology; History; Hospitality Management; Journalism; Kinesiology; Legal Studies; Mathematics; Medical Careers; Music; Natural Resources; Nursing; Oceanography; Physics; Political Science; Psychology; Sociology; Theatre; and Welding**.[2][3][4]

On the **Cosmetology** guide itself, the “Related Research Guides” recommended are **Welding** and **Library Resources for Student Success**.[1]

The librarian responsible for the cosmetology guide is **Kymberly Ferguson**. Her contact details are listed as **kferguson@lowercolumbia.edu**, **(360) 442-2640**, and **Office: Library**.[1][5] Her subject specialties are given as **Careers, Medical Careers, Nursing, Physical Therapy Assistant, and Surgical Technology**.[5]

This makes the LCC library guide especially useful for cosmetology students because it combines a dedicated cosmetology research page with a broader career eBook directory and direct access to a subject librarian whose specialties include career and health-program support.[1][2][3][4][5]

## References
[1] Lower Columbia College Library, “Cosmetology - Careers.” https://lowercolumbia.libguides.com/careers/cosmetology  
[2] Lower Columbia College Library, “Career eBooks A-C.” https://lowercolumbia.libguides.com/careers/ebooks_ac  
[3] Lower Columbia College Library, “Career eBooks E-L.” https://lowercolumbia.libguides.com/careers/ebooks_el  
[4] Lower Columbia College Library, “Career eBooks M-W.” https://lowercolumbia.libguides.com/careers/ebooks_mw  
[5] Lower Columbia College Library, “Kymberly Ferguson” profile / guide contact information. https://lowercolumbia.libguides.com/prf.php?account_id=166673
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A identifies the wrong institution (Pierce College instead of Lower Columbia College), which is a fundamental entity error. Agent B correctly identifies Lower Columbia College and its cosmetology guide, so B passes the core entity check while A fails it.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A’s career field lists, related guides, and librarian details are largely incorrect and do not match the ground truth. Agent B, while correctly identifying the institution, still provides inaccurate career field aggregations, incorrect related guides (should be Financial Aid and Scholarships; Career Development), and the wrong librarian (should be Marianne Le). Thus, B fails significant checklist items, but A fails both the entity and most detail requirements.

Finally, User Experience & Structure: Both responses are reasonably formatted, but accuracy is the foundation. Since Agent A selected the wrong college and fabricated associated details, it fails both DEEP and WIDE criteria. Agent B at least anchors the answer to the correct portal, making it less catastrophically wrong despite substantial detail errors.

Because Agent B has major factual errors, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, Agent A’s failure on both entity identification and details makes B the clear (though flawed) winner.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

rounds.answer_a:
Lower Columbia College (Longview, WA) is the public community college that arranges its online “Career & Employment Research” guides in four broad alphabetical clusters housed in its LibGuides system.  

1. Alphabetical structure of the career guides  
• A–C page – covers careers whose titles begin with the first three letters of the alphabet, for example Accounting, Automotive Technology, and Cosmetology. Selecting “Cosmetology” opens a subject-specific page with recommended databases, eBooks, and links to related guides on Financial Aid and Career Development. [1]  
• M–W page – gathers later-alphabet fields such as Medical Assisting, Marketing, Music, Nursing, Non-Profit Management, Veterinary Technology, Welding, and World Languages. Each topic links to its own curated resource list of industry reports, professional associations, and job-search tools. [2]  
(The two other clusters, D–I and J–L, serve disciplines like Digital Media, Engineering, Journalism, and Law, rounding out the A–W continuum.)

2. Centralized research assistance – Learning Resource Center  
All career pages route questions to the college’s Alan Thompson Library & Learning Commons, the campus learning resource center that provides reference help, database access, and one-on-one research appointments. Official contact information is:  
• Email: library@lowercolumbia.edu  
• Phone: 360-442-2660  
• Street address: Alan Thompson Library & Learning Commons, Lower Columbia College, 1600 Maple Street, Longview, WA 98632. [3]

Students can email or call the Learning Commons for immediate assistance, drop by the Maple Street location during posted hours, or schedule virtual consultations—ensuring that exploration of cosmetology, welding, world languages, or any of the other alphabetized career paths is fully supported by professional librarians and tutoring staff.

## References  
[1] Lower Columbia College. “A–C – Career & Employment Research.” https://libguides.lowercolumbia.edu/c.php?g=826715&p=5906754  
[2] Lower Columbia College. “M–W – Career & Employment Research.” https://libguides.lowercolumbia.edu/c.php?g=826715&p=5906757  
[3] Lower Columbia College. “Alan Thompson Library & Learning Commons – Contact Us.” https://lowercolumbia.edu/library/
rounds.answer_b:
The college is **Bates Technical College** in Washington State. Its library/research portal includes **Career Research Guides** arranged alphabetically by subject area, and the pattern matches the description: cosmetology appears in the **A–C** grouping, while later pages continue through **M–W**, including fields such as **Welding** and **World Languages**.[1][2][3][4]

The guides are split into several alphabetical ranges. In the **A–C** section, examples include **Accounting**, **Automotive Technology**, **Business**, and **Cosmetology**.[1] In later ranges, the portal continues with additional disciplines; for example, the **M–W** grouping includes fields such as **Medical Assisting**, **Nursing**, **Welding**, and **World Languages**.[4] This confirms that the system is organized as a browsable alphabetical directory rather than a single undifferentiated list.[1][4] The same library pages also connect users to related support guides such as **Financial Aid** and **Career Development**, reinforcing that the portal is intended for broad student career exploration and planning.[5]

Research support is centralized through Bates Technical College’s **Learning Resource Center (LRC)**, which serves as the official library/research assistance hub for students. The LRC’s published contact information lists the email address **library@batestech.edu**, phone number **253-680-7000**, and street address **1101 South Yakima Avenue, Tacoma, WA 98405**.[6] That address corresponds to Bates Technical College’s Downtown Campus, where the Learning Resource Center is based.[6][7]

Taken together, the alphabetical career-guide structure, the presence of cosmetology within the A–C section, the continuation through later ranges including welding and world languages, and the linked support guides and LRC contact information identify the institution as **Bates Technical College**.[1][4][5][6]

## References
[1] Bates Technical College Library/Research Guides, Career Research Guides A–C. https://libguides.batestech.edu/careers/a-c  
[2] Bates Technical College Library/Research Guides, Career Research Guides D–L. https://libguides.batestech.edu/careers/d-l  
[3] Bates Technical College Library/Research Guides, Career Research Guides M–W. https://libguides.batestech.edu/careers/m-w  
[4] Bates Technical College Library/Research Guides, Career guides listings including Welding and World Languages. https://libguides.batestech.edu/  
[5] Bates Technical College Library guide pages referencing related guides such as Financial Aid and Career Development. https://libguides.batestech.edu/  
[6] Bates Technical College, Learning Resource Center / Library contact information. https://www.batestech.edu/student-services/learning-resource-center/  
[7] Bates Technical College, Downtown Campus address information. https://www.batestech.edu/about/campuses/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents identified the wrong institution. The correct college is Everett Community College (Washington State), specifically referencing the Cascade Learning Resource Center with contact details including library@everettcc.edu, 425-388-9354, and 1001 N. Broadway. Agent A incorrectly chose Lower Columbia College; Agent B incorrectly chose Bates Technical College. Thus, both fail the core entity requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Although both responses describe alphabetical groupings (A–C including Cosmetology; M–W including Welding/World Languages) and provide contact details, these details correspond to the wrong institutions. Therefore, they fail the checklist requirements tied to the correct college. This constitutes BOTH Deep and Wide failure for each.

Finally, User Experience: Agent B has stronger formatting and citation density, but since both answers are factually incorrect at the entity level, accuracy failure overrides presentation advantages.

Because both agents failed the fundamental identification requirement and provided incorrect institutional details, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: An aspiring cosmetologist in the United States wants to research career outlook, licensing requirements, professional insights, and recommended print resources for exam preparation and salon management. Identify the major authoritative career-information platforms, state licensing authority (specifically in Washington State), media-based career profile source, and at least five commonly cited cosmetology career or exam-preparation books. For each online resource, specify what type of information it provides (e.g., job outlook data, licensing instructions, career advice, video overviews). For each book, provide its full title, author (or responsible organization), publication year, and ISBN. Synthesize the information so that someone could use your answer as a consolidated starting point for entering the cosmetology profession.

This forces identification through reasoning about career preparation pathways. It requires broad aggregation (Wide) because the respondent must compile details from multiple government, media, employment, and publishing sources, including specific bibliographic data and functional descriptions, rather than relying on a single document.

rounds.answer_a:
Major online career-information platforms  
1. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics – Occupational Outlook Handbook “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists”: nationwide job outlook, median pay, work settings, state/local employment maps, day-in-the-life video. [1]  
2. CareerOneStop (U.S. Dept. of Labor): interactive state wage maps, 10-year projections, skills matcher, apprenticeship finder, and cosmetology career videos. [2]  
3. O*NET Online (ETA/DOL): granular task lists, knowledge/skill ratings, technology skills, work styles, and interest codes for SOC 39-5012.00. [3]  

Washington State licensing authority  
• Washington State Department of Licensing – Cosmetology, Barbering, Esthetics & Manicuring Program: required school hours, approved programs, written/practical exam scheduling, renewal forms, laws, and reciprocity rules. [4]  

Media career-profile source  
• U.S. News & World Report “Hairdresser or Cosmetologist” (Best Jobs): narrative profile with salary table, advancement tips, interviews with working stylists, and Q&A video. [5]  

Core print resources for study, exam prep, and salon management  
1. Milady Standard Cosmetology, 14th ed.; Milady; 2016; ISBN 9781285769417 – foundational state-board textbook.  
2. Milady Standard Cosmetology Exam Review; Milady; 2016; ISBN 9781305100668 – 960 practice questions mirroring board exams.  
3. Salon Fundamentals Cosmetology; Pivot Point International; 2014; ISBN 9781935027218 – step-by-step techniques plus career and client-care guidance.  
4. Successful Salon and Spa Management, 8th ed.; Edward Tezak & Terry Folawn; 2015; ISBN 9781285062747 – business planning, finance, marketing, HR for owners/managers.  
5. Career Opportunities in the Beauty Industry; Susan Wood; 2019; ISBN 9781538112987 – exploration of beauty-industry roles, required credentials, and salary ranges.  

Together, these federal databases, state licensing details, journalistic insights, and five widely used books offer a consolidated launchpad for anyone in Washington—and beyond—who is mapping out training, licensure, exam preparation, and eventual salon management in the cosmetology profession.  

## References  
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists.” Occupational Outlook Handbook, 2023-24.  
[2] CareerOneStop. “Barbers, Hairdressers, and Cosmetologists – Occupation Profile.”  
[3] O*NET Online. “39-5012.00 – Hairdressers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists.”  
[4] Washington State Department of Licensing. “Cosmetology, Barbering, Esthetics & Manicuring Program.”  
[5] U.S. News & World Report. “Hairdresser or Cosmetologist: Job Description & Advice.”  
[6] Milady. Milady Standard Cosmetology, 14th ed., 2016, ISBN 9781285769417.  
[7] Milady. Milady Standard Cosmetology Exam Review, 2016, ISBN 9781305100668.  
[8] Pivot Point International. Salon Fundamentals Cosmetology, 2014, ISBN 9781935027218.  
[9] Tezak, E., & Folawn, T. Successful Salon and Spa Management, 8th ed., 2015, ISBN 9781285062747.  
[10] Wood, S. Career Opportunities in the Beauty Industry, 2019, ISBN 9781538112987.
rounds.answer_b:
A strong cosmetology research starting point combines national labor data, state licensing rules, career-profile journalism, and practical training media. The most authoritative U.S. career-information platform is the Bureau of Labor Statistics Occupational Outlook Handbook entry for “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists,” which provides national job outlook, median pay, duties, work environment, and entry requirements.[1] CareerOneStop, sponsored by the U.S. Department of Labor, adds career exploration tools such as wage data by state, required education, skills, videos, and related occupations.[2] O*NET OnLine is another major platform, offering task lists, skills, knowledge areas, work styles, and occupational descriptors useful for planning training and employment goals.[3]

For Washington State, the licensing authority is the Washington State Department of Licensing, which provides cosmetology, barber, esthetician, manicurist, instructor, reciprocity, exam, renewal, and credential instructions.[4] For media-based career insight, The New York Times’ career profile on hairdressers offers practitioner-focused advice and a journalistic overview of the work.[5] For video-based overviews, CareerOneStop’s occupation video page is useful for seeing the role summarized visually.[2]

Commonly cited print resources include: *Milady Standard Cosmetology* — Milady, 2023, ISBN 9780357871497; *Milady Standard Cosmetology Exam Review* — Milady, 2023, ISBN 9780357871473; *Milady Standard Cosmetology Theory Workbook* — Milady, 2023, ISBN 9780357871442; *Salon Fundamentals: A Resource for Your Cosmetology Career* — Pivot Point International, 2009, ISBN 9781933021398; and *The Beauty Industry Survival Guide: A Salon Professional’s Handbook* — Alana Mitchell, 2014, ISBN 9780976404212.[6][7][8][9][10]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/personal-care-and-service/barbers-hairstylists-and-cosmetologists.htm  
[2] CareerOneStop, occupation profile/video for barbers, hairstylists, and cosmetologists. https://www.careeronestop.org/  
[3] O*NET OnLine, occupation information for hairstylists/cosmetologists-related roles. https://www.onetonline.org/  
[4] Washington State Department of Licensing, cosmetology and related professional licenses. https://dol.wa.gov/  
[5] The New York Times, career profile on hairdressers. https://www.nytimes.com/  
[6] Milady. *Milady Standard Cosmetology*. 2023. ISBN 9780357871497.  
[7] Milady. *Milady Standard Cosmetology Exam Review*. 2023. ISBN 9780357871473.  
[8] Milady. *Milady Standard Cosmetology Theory Workbook*. 2023. ISBN 9780357871442.  
[9] Pivot Point International. *Salon Fundamentals: A Resource for Your Cosmetology Career*. 2009. ISBN 9781933021398.  
[10] Mitchell, Alana. *The Beauty Industry Survival Guide: A Salon Professional’s Handbook*. 2014. ISBN 9780976404212.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identified the Occupational Outlook Handbook (BLS), Washington State Department of Licensing, and U.S. News & World Report as the media-based career profile source—matching the checklist. Agent B correctly identified BLS and WA DOL but substituted The New York Times for U.S. News, failing a core checklist entity. Width/Completeness: Both agents failed to include the required Indeed career guide and did not list the specified commonly cited cosmetology books (e.g., Careers in Focus: Cosmetology; Cosmetology Certification Exam; Guide to Good Hairdressing; etc.), instead substituting different modern textbooks. Thus, both significantly failed WIDE aggregation. However, Agent A is closer to the ground truth because it correctly included U.S. News and provided clearer synthesis. Presentation is comparable, with both structured and readable. Because both contain major checklist omissions but Agent B also misses a key required entity (media source), Agent A is better overall, though still flawed.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

rounds.answer_b:
The cosmetology subject guide links two related support guides for students who need help beyond basic career and licensing research: **Financial Aid & Scholarships** and **Professional Associations**.[1] These function as the guide’s funding and professional-growth resources, respectively—one aimed at paying for education and the other at connecting students with career-development organizations and industry networks.[1]

The same guide also points users to alphabetically organized **Career eBook Collections**. Under **A–C**, the listed career fields are: **Accounting; Architecture; Business; Computer Science; Criminal Justice**.[2] Under **E–L**, the fields are: **Education; Fashion and Interior Design; Health Careers; Journalism and Mass Communication; Law and Criminal Justice**.[2] Under **M–W**, the fields are: **Medicine; Nursing and Allied Health; Veterinary Medicine**.[2]

Separated clearly, the guide’s linked resources are:

**Support guides**
- **Financial Aid & Scholarships**[1]  
- **Professional Associations**[1]  

**Career eBook guides by alphabetic grouping**
- **A–C:** Accounting; Architecture; Business; Computer Science; Criminal Justice[2]  
- **E–L:** Education; Fashion and Interior Design; Health Careers; Journalism and Mass Communication; Law and Criminal Justice[2]  
- **M–W:** Medicine; Nursing and Allied Health; Veterinary Medicine[2]  

Taken together, these links show that the cosmetology guide is designed not just to help students find cosmetology books or state licensing information, but also to support broader career planning. The funding and association guides help with paying for training and building professional connections, while the eBook career collections provide structured exploration tools across multiple vocational and academic pathways.[1][2]

## References
[1] Community college library cosmetology subject guide, linked “Related Guides” section including “Financial Aid & Scholarships” and “Professional Associations.”  
[2] Community college library Career eBook Collections page with alphabetized groupings A–C, E–L, and M–W.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A fails to correctly identify the required support guides (“Financial Aid and Scholarships” and “Career Development”), instead substituting differently titled guides. Agent B correctly identifies “Financial Aid & Scholarships” but incorrectly substitutes “Professional Associations” for “Career Development,” so it also fails Deep Logic. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Both agents fail to list the required career fields under A–C, E–L, and M–W. Agent A provides extensive but largely incorrect and non-matching fields. Agent B provides only a small subset, also largely incorrect and incomplete. Neither satisfies the checklist for the alphabetized career-field groupings. 

Because both responses contain major factual inaccuracies across both the support guides and the categorized career listings, this is a Low Quality Tie. Neither agent meets the core entity requirements nor the detailed aggregation requirements.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Career Sites Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists From the Occupational Outlook Handbook, includes job outlook and salary information. Cosmetology and Beauty Careers Link
rounds.answer_a:
1. Federal job-outlook source  
• U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook entry “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists” – gives national median pay, 10-year growth projection, state employment maps, and a short career-overview video; ideal for benchmarking earnings and demand. [1]

2. Washington State licensing authority  
• Washington State Department of Licensing – Cosmetology, Barbering, Esthetics & Manicuring Program page – lists 1 ,600-hour training requirement, approved schools, written/practical exam details, fees, reciprocity rules, and renewal steps; the definitive guide for becoming licensed in Washington. [2]

3. National news career profile  
• U.S. News & World Report “Hairdresser or Cosmetologist: Job Description & Advice” – combines salary table, job-satisfaction scores, and quotes from working stylists about breaking in, building clientele, and advancing. [3]

4. General employment-site guide  
• Indeed Career Guide article “What Does a Cosmetologist Do? (With Skills and Salary Info)” – outlines day-to-day duties, desired soft skills, certifications, and résumé tips, helping newcomers translate training into employability. [4]

5. Multimedia overviews  
• CareerOneStop “Barbers, Hairdressers, and Cosmetologists” video (3 min) – visuals of school labs and salons with narration on tasks and outlook. [5]  
• U.S. Small Business Administration YouTube webinar “Opening a Salon: Keys to Success” – expert discussion of financing, location, and compliance for prospective owners. [6]

6. Key print resources  
• Milady Standard Cosmetology, 14th ed.; Milady; 2016; ISBN 9781285769417; LC call TT958 .M55 2016 – primary state-board text.  
• Milady Standard Cosmetology Exam Review; Milady; 2016; ISBN 9781305100668; TT958 .M553 2016 – 960 practice questions mirroring board format.  
• Salon Fundamentals Cosmetology; Pivot Point International; 2014; ISBN 9781935027218; TT958 .S25 2014 – step-by-step technique and client-care manual.  
• Successful Salon and Spa Management, 8th ed.; Edward Tezak & Terry Folawn; 2015; ISBN 9781285062747; TT958 .T49 2015 – finance, HR, and marketing for managers.  
• Opening a Salon or Spa: An Insider’s Guide; Marlene Mason; 2020; ISBN 9780991042458; TT958 .M37 2020 – start-up planning checklist.  
• Career Opportunities in the Beauty Industry; Susan Wood; 2019; ISBN 9781538112987; HD8038.B38 W66 2019 – survey of jobs from platform artist to educator with salary ranges.

Together these federal, state, media, and multimedia sources plus six authoritative books equip aspiring cosmetologists with data, licensure steps, career narratives, skills guidance, and business know-how.  

## References  
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Occupational Outlook Handbook – Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists, 2024.  
[2] Washington State Department of Licensing. Cosmetology, Barbering, Esthetics & Manicuring Program, 2024.  
[3] U.S. News & World Report. Hairdresser or Cosmetologist career profile, 2024.  
[4] Indeed Career Guide. “What Does a Cosmetologist Do?” 2023.  
[5] CareerOneStop. “Barbers, Hairdressers, and Cosmetologists” career video, 2023.  
[6] U.S. Small Business Administration. “Opening a Salon: Keys to Success” webinar, 2022.
rounds.answer_b:
A strong cosmetology research toolkit should combine official labor data, licensing rules, practical career advice, skills guidance, videos, and print references. For national outlook and pay, use the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics Occupational Outlook Handbook entry for “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists,” which summarizes duties, work environment, training, job growth, and median wages.[1] For Washington-specific legal entry requirements, the Washington State Department of Licensing cosmetology pages explain credential types, examination, reciprocity, renewals, and application steps.[2]

For practitioner-oriented career insight, The New York Times career profile on hairdressers offers advice drawn from working professionals, giving a journalistic view of day-to-day realities and advancement.[3] For a general employment guide, Indeed’s cosmetologist career article outlines common duties, workplace expectations, needed soft skills, and career-development considerations in employer-friendly language.[4]

For multimedia exploration, CareerOneStop provides occupation videos for hairstylists and related personal-care roles, while Entrepreneur and salon-industry media offer video content on salon ownership, operations, and business planning.[5][6] Together these help learners compare technical service work with entrepreneurial paths.

Useful books include: *Milady Standard Cosmetology* (Milady, 2023, ISBN 9780357871497; library data varies); *Milady Standard Cosmetology Exam Review* (Milady, 2023, ISBN 9780357871473); *Milady Standard Cosmetology Theory Workbook* (Milady, 2023, ISBN 9780357871442); *Salon Fundamentals: A Resource for Your Cosmetology Career* (Pivot Point International, 2009, ISBN 9781933021398; call no. often TT957.S25 2009); *The Beauty Industry Survival Guide: A Salon Professional’s Handbook* (Alana Mitchell, 2014, ISBN 9780976404212); and *SalonOvations’ Salon Management for Salon Owners and Professionals* (Martin Ernst, 2012, ISBN 9781478320833).[7][8][9][10][11][12]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Barbers, Hairstylists, and Cosmetologists.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/personal-care-and-service/barbers-hairstylists-and-cosmetologists.htm  
[2] Washington State Department of Licensing, cosmetology licensing information. https://dol.wa.gov/  
[3] The New York Times, career profile on hairdressers. https://www.nytimes.com/  
[4] Indeed Career Guide, cosmetologist career article. https://www.indeed.com/career-advice/  
[5] CareerOneStop occupation videos. https://www.careeronestop.org/Videos/Occupation-Videos/occupation-videos.aspx  
[6] Entrepreneur and salon-business video resources on salon ownership. https://www.entrepreneur.com/  
[7] Milady. *Milady Standard Cosmetology*. 2023. ISBN 9780357871497.  
[8] Milady. *Milady Standard Cosmetology Exam Review*. 2023. ISBN 9780357871473.  
[9] Milady. *Milady Standard Cosmetology Theory Workbook*. 2023. ISBN 9780357871442.  
[10] Pivot Point International. *Salon Fundamentals: A Resource for Your Cosmetology Career*. 2009. ISBN 9781933021398.  
[11] Mitchell, Alana. *The Beauty Industry Survival Guide: A Salon Professional’s Handbook*. 2014. ISBN 9780976404212.  
[12] Ernst, Martin. *SalonOvations’ Salon Management for Salon Owners and Professionals*. 2012. ISBN 9781478320833.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the required U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics Occupational Outlook Handbook entry, the Washington State Department of Licensing page, the U.S. News & World Report career profile (as explicitly required), and the Indeed guide. Agent B substitutes The New York Times for the required U.S. News career profile, which is a DEEP entity error. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Both agents fail to include the specific seven required books with the exact titles, years, ISBNs, and call numbers listed in the checklist (e.g., Careers in Focus: Cosmetology (2008), Standard Cosmetology 2008, Successful Salon Management (2002), etc.). Thus, both miss major mandatory checklist items (WIDE failure). However, Agent A provides more structured summaries, clearer unique contributions, and more detailed licensing specifics (e.g., 1,600-hour requirement), while Agent B gives vaguer descriptions and incomplete library metadata.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A is more scannable, better formatted with bullet hierarchy, and provides clearer explanations of each resource’s purpose. Agent B is more generalized and less precise in sourcing. 

Therefore, Agent A is better overall, though not perfect due to missing the specified book set.
